coredata.dc:title: Inkjet printing based ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesis for glutathione sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Zhaoxuan
Zhou, Ziping
Zhang, Weifei
Zhang, Xueji
Lin, Jin-Ming
coredata.dc:description: Manganese dioxide (MnO2) with small size is competent in sensing applications, but its synthesis generally adopts templates or in complex ways. Inkjet printing technique with excellent performance offers a versatile tool due to its stability, flexibility, economy. Herein, an inkjet printing method was developed for rapid synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets. The findings validated the feasibility of inkjet printing method for MnO2 nanosheets synthesis and achieved the demand of small size and facile mode. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets in glutathione (GSH) sensing achieved 0.26 μM, which was about 40% more sensitive than that of the typical MnO2 nanosheets, enabling the establishment of a rapid and efficient modality for sensitive and selective GSH sensing. By virtue of the inkjet printing approach, the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was obtained in a short time without complicated fabricating process. It can be foreseen that the proposed inkjet printing approach would facilitate the application prospects of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets in diverse fields. Such a facile approach may open new avenues for synthesis of ultra-small or ultrafine nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inkjet printing
Ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets
Glutathione
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21727814 , 81872829 , 21621003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing method and the principle of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets for GSH sensing. (not real scale).

(a) Relationship between pulse voltage and droplet diameter generated by the inkjet printing system. (b) The resulting mixture was encapsulated in monodisperse droplets generated by inkjet printing system. The scale bar is 30 μm. (c) TEM image of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized in droplets. The scale bar is 50 nm. (d) TEM image of typical MnO2 nanosheets. The scale bar is 500 nm. (e) XPS of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized in droplets. (f–g) Fluorescence spectra of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets and typical MnO2 nanosheets. (h–i) DLS of typical MnO2 nanosheets and ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets.
(a) TEM image of the CQDs. The scale bar is 20 nm. (b) UV–vis spectra of the CQDs. (b) UV–vis spectra of the CQDs after assembling with ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets.

(a) Fluorescence responses of CQDs with the addition of different ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets concentration. (b) Fluorescence of CQDs quenched by different concentrations of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets. (c) Fluorescence restoration of the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites in the presence of GSH. (d) The relationship between the relative fluorescence of the two types of nanocomposites and the additive GSH concentration. F0: fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite; F: fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite in the presence of GSH. Red line: relationship between the relative fluorescence of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites and GSH concentration. Black line: relationship between the relative fluorescence of typical MnO2-CQDs nanocomposite and GSH concentration. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Fluorescence response of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites toward GSH and interfering substances. (F0: the fluorescence response of water; F: the fluorescence response of the target (GSH) or interfering substances).

head.ce:title: Inkjet printing based ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesis for glutathione sensing

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Bioengineering and Sensing Technology
School of Chemistry and Biological Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Manganese dioxide (MnO2) with small size is competent in sensing applications, but its synthesis generally adopts templates or in complex ways. Inkjet printing technique with excellent performance offers a versatile tool due to its stability, flexibility, economy. Herein, an inkjet printing method was developed for rapid synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets. The findings validated the feasibility of inkjet printing method for MnO2 nanosheets synthesis and achieved the demand of small size and facile mode. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets in glutathione (GSH) sensing achieved 0.26 μM, which was about 40% more sensitive than that of the typical MnO2 nanosheets, enabling the establishment of a rapid and efficient modality for sensitive and selective GSH sensing. By virtue of the inkjet printing approach, the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was obtained in a short time without complicated fabricating process. It can be foreseen that the proposed inkjet printing approach would facilitate the application prospects of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets in diverse fields. Such a facile approach may open new avenues for synthesis of ultra-small or ultrafine nanomaterials.

An inkjet printing method for rapid synthesizing ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was developed and the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets based GSH sensing exhibited high sensitivity.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Reactions to obtain nanomaterials of several nanometres in size are generally performed in tubes or flasks. Whereas the transition from chemistry-in-tube to chemistry-in-droplet benefits from the opportunities offered by the droplet generation techniques [1]. Droplet-based system renders reactions carried out at a small scale by compartmentalizing the reaction volume from microliter down to even femtoliter, which has gained tremendous development due to its advantages of compartmentalization, miniaturization, and parallelization [2]. Droplet-based system bears vital value in the application of nucleic acid quantification [3–5] and single cell analysis [6]. In addition, droplets that can be precisely controlled potentially expand their function as synthesis microreactors [7]. Printing techniques, particularly inkjet printing, can eject liquid droplets in a drop-on-demand (DOD) manner during the course of droplet generation to precisely produce monodisperse droplets with specific volume and velocity control, thereby becomes an alternative pathway to fabricate ultra-thin or ultra-small nanomaterials [8–10].

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets with unique properties of high specific surface area and high fluorescence quenching efficiency has aroused considerable interests, which have facilitated their applications in sensing [11,12], cell imaging [13] and biomedicine [14]. Previous reports have revealed that the applications of MnO2 nanomaterials with small size in cell imaging [15,16], drug delivery [17] and sensing [18]. However, the fabrication of MnO2 nanosheets with smaller size still remains a challenge that may seriously limit its applications. In current status, most of MnO2 nanosheets with smaller size are synthesized using templates or in complex ways, which is time consuming and inefficient. Therefore, continuous effort is still necessary to implement simple and convenient methods for synthesizing MnO2 nanosheets with small size. To the best of our knowldege, only very limited researches are involved in inkjet printing in the manufacture of small size MnO2 nanosheets. To fulfill the blank, piezoelectric inkjet printing was employed in this work for the execution of micro-manufacturing.

Herein, an inkjet printing strategy was developed for rapid synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets and the synthetic ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets were validated in GSH sensing with high sensitivity. The experimental procedure was illustrated in Fig. 1 . The ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was obtained through the inkjet printing approach. Printing the reaction raw materials into nanoliter droplets greatly reduced the reaction volume compared with the traditional reaction in a round-bottom flask, thus limiting the growth of MnO2 nanosheets during the synthesis process. Moreover, the limited supply of raw materials in printed nanoliter droplets makes it possible to synthesize small size MnO2 nanosheet. MnCl2 was oxidized by H2O2 in the presence of tetramethylammonium hydroxide. The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was prepared in advance and incubated with the synthetic ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, the fluorescence emission of CQDs was quenched due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [19]. Afterwards, the introduction of GSH recovers the fluorescence of CQDs. Therefore, the concentration of GSH can be assessed by monitoring the fluorescence recovery. The findings demonstrated the feasibility of inkjet printing method for ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesis. In comparation with the conventional reaction in round-bottomed flask, the reaction materials printed into nanoliter droplets by inkjet printing method greatly shortens the synthesis time with a facile and rapid mode.

Sodium chloride (NaCl), Calcium chloride (CaCl2), Potassium chloride (KCl), L-alanine (Ala), L-arginine (Arg), L-asparagine monohydrate (Asn), L-aspartic acid (Asp), L-glutamine (Gln), L-glycine (Gly), L-isoleucine (Ile), L-leucine (Leu), L-lysine (Lys), L-methionine (Met), L-phenylalanine (Phe), L-serine (Ser), L-tryptophan (Trp), L-valine (Val), bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. MnCl2·4H2O, tetramethylammonium hydroxide, H2O2 (30 wt% in H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was obtained by Ultrapure Millipore system.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was performed on a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra was recorded on UV-1800 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra was measured with a F-7000 spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra was recorded on a VG Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Model ESCALab220i-XL). The zeta potentials and Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on a Zetasizer laser light scattering system (NanoZS90, Malvern Instruments Corporation, England).

Proper pretreatment of inkjet chip is a prerequisite for droplets stability and reliable results. Basically, the inkjet chip was cleaned with alcohol and ultrapure water to eliminate bubbles in the channel, followed by drying with air. The new hematocrit tube and fused quartz capillary was rinsed thoroughly with alcohol and ultrapure water, followed by drying with air. After that, inkjet sampling solution was introduced into the channel of inkjet chip through hematocrit tube.

In a 2 mL centrifuge tube, 0.25 mL of MnCl2·4H2O aqueous solution (0.3 M) was mixed with 0.5 mL of tetramethylammonium hydroxide aqueous solution (0.6 M) containing 3 wt% of H2O2 (30 wt% in H2O) within 15 s. The resulting mixture was rapidly transferred as inkjet sample into a 75 mm hematocrit tube and then introduced into the inkjet chip. The inkjet print system was employed to produce monodisperse droplets, which were collected in the centrifuge tube kept for 4 h after formation in ambient temperature. Afterwards the droplets were demulsified by high-speed centrifugation until disappearance. At this time, there was an obvious delamination in the centrifuge tube, the upper solution was discarded, the bottom brown solution was reserved for further use. Typical MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized using a general method previously reported [20]. Briefly, the resulting mixture was stirred vigorously in a round-bottomed flask for 12 h at room temperature.

The CQDs was synthesized according to the previously reported method with some modification [21]. In brief, the 50 mg BSA proteins was denatured in 10 mL ultrapure water under gentle stirring for 5 min. Then the mixture was transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The resultant production was further purified by centrifugation at 15,000g for 10 min to remove the large particles. The purified CQDs was obtained and kept at room temperature for further use.

The ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets solution of various concentrations (0–90 μg/mL, respectively) was incubated with the CQDs aqueous solution for the yielding of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites. For GSH sensing, 999 μL of the resultant ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites solutions was mixed with 1 μL GSH solution of different concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 mM. Afterwards, the mixtures were separately transferred into 1 cm quartz cuvettes to record the fluorescence emission spectra with an excitation wavelength at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of preparing droplets encapsulating MnO2 nanosheets by inkjet printing method, the solution containing the preparing raw materials needs to be introduced into the inkjet chip. Therefore, the synthesis reaction of MnO2 nanosheets should be as gentle as possible. In order to make the reaction process gentle, the oxidation reaction of MnCl2 was selected for MnO2 nanosheets synthesis instead of KMnO4 reduction. Inkjet printing system has the inherent capability to efficiently generate large numbers of droplets, thus encapsulating the preparing raw materials at the beginning of the reaction for subsequent synthesis. The superior performance of inkjet printing system to produce monodisperse droplets has been experimented by Lin's team [22,23]. Monodisperse droplets generation employing inkjet printing system can be achieved by controlling the driving conditions (pulse voltage and pulse width) of piezoelectric actuator on the inkjet chip. The size of droplet could be controlled by adjusting the value of pulse voltage and pulse width. Pulse voltage is the value of driving voltage on piezoelectric actuator, and pulse width is the adding time of voltage on piezoelectric actuator. The relationship of pulse voltage and droplets diameter was shown in Fig. 2 a. Under the optimized conditions, the pulse voltage of 40 V and pulse width of 20 μs were set to inkjet the resulting solution. The resulting solution was used as dispersed phase, and the mineral oil supplemented with surfactant was used as continuous phase to form water-in-oil droplets. Process of the droplets generation can be found in Video 1, which was recorded by high-speed microscope. Fig. 2b showed the resulting solution encapsulated in droplets generated by inkjet printing method. After demulsification by high-speed centrifugation, the brown suspension was obtained and characterized by TEM (Fig. 2c). The MnO2 nanosheets obtained by inkjet printing method exhibited an ultra-small sheet-like morphology with a diameter of approximate 120 nm, confirming the successful synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets. Meanwhile, typical MnO2 nanosheets was synthesized by stirring the resulting solution in a round-bottomed flask for 12 h using the general method. As shown in Fig. 2d, the TEM image presented the morphology of typical MnO2 nanosheets with a much larger diameter, reaching over 1000 nm. To further confirm the successful synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, XPS and UV–vis absorption spectra of the brown solution were measured. As shown in Fig. 2e, XPS data exhibited the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 peaks at around 653.2 eV and 654.1 eV respectively, characterizing the +4 valence state of Mn [24]. Fig. 2f displayed the UV–vis absorption spectrum of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing method. It can be observed that the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets present a wide absorption band from 300 nm to 600 nm with a peak located at 380 nm, corresponding to the optical characteristics reported by general method (Fig. 2g). Furthermore, DLS measurements were supplemented to characterize the size distribution of MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by different methods. As exhibited in Fig. 2h and i, the DLS spectra indicated that the size of MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by the inkjet printing method was significantly smaller than that of general method, evidentially demonstrating the size superiority of the inkjet printing method. Lastly, as exhibited in Table 1 , the zeta potential of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was - 40.6 mV, indicating the negative surface charge of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets and their good dispersibility in aqueous solution. The above results validated the feasibility of inkjet printing method for MnO2 nanosheets synthesis with small size, which could be subjected to further applications.

The CQDs were prepared in accordance with the method described in previous work [21], presenting the advantages of highly water-dispersible and stability at room temperature [25–29]. The size and microstructure of the CQDs were analysed by employing TEM, it showed the as-prepared CQDs had a regular spherical shape with a diameter ranging from 2 nm to 6 nm (Fig. 3 a). Next, the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites were yielded via the incubation of CQDs and the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, which could be evidenced by comparing the UV–vis spectra before and after assembly. Before assembly, the UV–vis absorption peak of CQDs was around 550 nm (Fig. 3b), while after assembling with ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, the absorption peak appeared shift (Fig. 3c). These supplementary characterization measurements supported the successful preparation of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites. It has been reported that MnO2 nanosheets were highly efficient quenchers with a rich surface area, benefiting for the CQDs deposition on their surface [30–32]. In addition, the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets is negatively charged due to the presence of manganese vacancies on the surface and with this property can easily adsorb CQDs with positive charge. Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching characteristics of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets toward CQDs were investigated. Different concentration of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets were introduced to optimize the quenching efficiency and achieve the best quenching performance. As expected, the fluorescence of CQDs was gradually quenched as the concentration of the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets increasing from 0 to 90 μg/mL, indicating that the binding of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets and CQDs could be more fully realized with the increase of concentration of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 4 a). Since the CQDs in solution is deposited on the surface of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, FRET occurs from CQDs to ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets, such a mechanism causes the fluorescence emission of CQDs to be effectively quenched. Additionally, in Fig. 4b, when the concentration of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets was 60 μg/mL, the fluorescence decline curve reached an inflection point and fluorescence quenching efficiency of the system is not significantly improved even at a higher concentration. It can be concluded that the optimal concentration of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets for GSH sensing was 60 μg/mL. Therefore, 60 μg/mL ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets were used for the subsequent experiment.

Next the capability of the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites for GSH sensing was investigated by introducing various concentrations of GSH. During this process, the oxidation-reduction reaction of GSH and MnO2 nanosheets inhibited the FRET effect due to the disappearance of MnO2. Therefore, the kinetic behaviour of GSH reacting with the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence recovery. As shown in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites gradually recovers with the increase of additive GSH concentration, and the restored fluorescence was dependent on the additive concentration of GSH. When the concentration of GSH increased to 200 μM, no further increase of fluorescence intensity was observed, revealing that the fluorescence signal reached maximum. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites and typical MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites incubated with different concentrations of GSH was compared. In both types of the nanocomposites, the oxidation-reduction reaction between GSH and MnO2 nanosheets triggers MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ and the decomposition the MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites. In addition, the smaller size of MnO2 nanosheets has higher specific surface area resulted in more rapid and full reaction with GSH, efficiently improving the GSH detection sensitivity. The relative fluorescence intensity between GSH concentration and both types of nanocomposites were calculated and shown in Fig. 4d. The fitting curve (red line) helps to the quantification of GSH with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. According to the definition of three times the deviation of the blank signal (3s), the limit of detection (LOD) for GSH is calculated to be as low as 0.26 μM using the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing method. While the LOD for GSH was 0.36 μM (black line) using the typical MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by general method. After comparison with typical MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by general method, it can be implied that usage of relatively large MnO2 nanosheet leads to the decrease of detection sensitivity, the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets based GSH sensing improves nearly 40% in detection sensitivity. Overall, the inkjet printing method used for the synthesis of ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets has achieved the demand of small size and facile mode and the synthetic ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets were validated in GSH sensing with high sensitivity.

For a sensor, selectivity is also a critical factor to be taken into consideration. To evaluate the selectivity of the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites toward GSH, the fluorescence responses of other possible interfering substances were investigated, including some metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+), amino acids (L-alanine, L-arginine, L-asparagine monohydrate, L-aspartic acid, L-glutamine, L-glycine, L-isoleucine, L-leucine, L-lysine, L-methionine, L-phenylalanine, L-serine, L-tryptophan, L-valine) and protein (BSA). The experimental results were shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 exhibited a remarkable enhancement in the presence of GSH. In marked contrast, the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 displayed no obvious changes after the addition of investigated interfering substances. Hence, the fluorescence enhancement of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites toward GSH was much higher than other investigated interferences. Overall, the ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites could be implemented for selectively sensing of GSH.

In summary, a facile and convenient strategy for ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesis based on inkjet printing was developed. The as-synthesized ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets exhibited the same characteristics with typical MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by general method, confirming the feasibility of inkjet printing method. According to the fluorescence recovery mechanism of ultra-small MnO2-CQDs nanocomposites caused by GSH, ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets based GSH sensing was more sensitive than that of typical MnO2 nanosheets, indicating that ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets synthesized by inkjet printing method enables rapid and sensitive GSH sensing. Given its rapid synthesis feature and facile operation mode, we believe that the ultra-small MnO2 nanosheets fabricated by inkjet printing strategy will expand the application prospects in the fields of biosensing, nanomedicine, biochemistry. Moreover, this work may open up new possibilities for developing inkjet printing platforms for ultra-small or ultrafine nanomaterial synthesis.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21727814, 81872829, 21621003).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.121989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Droplet microfluidics: a tool for biology, chemistry and nanotechnology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Mashaghi, A. Abbaspourrad, D.A. Weitz, A.M. van Oijen, Droplet microfluidics: A tool for biology, chemistry and nanotechnology, TrAC-Trends Anal. Chem. 82 (2016) 118-125.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reactions in droplets in microfluidic channels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Song, D.L. Chen, R.F. Ismagilov, Reactions in droplets in microfluidic channels, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 45 (2006) 7336-7356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiplex enrichment and detection of rare KRAS mutations in liquid biopsy samples using digital droplet pre-amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.D. Pratt, R.W. Cowan, S.L. Manning, E. Qiao, H. Cameron, K. Schradle, D.M. Simeone, D.B. Zhen, Multiplex enrichment and detection of rare KRAS mutations in liquid biopsy samples using digital droplet pre-amplification, Anal. Chem. 91(2019) 7516-7523.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Absolute quantification of lung cancer related microRNA by droplet digital PCR

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wang, F. Jing, G. Li, Z. Wu, Z. Cheng, J. Zhang, H. Zhang, C. Jia, Q. Jin, H. Mao, Absolute quantification of lung cancer related microRNA by droplet digital PCR, Biosens. Bioelectron. 74 (2015) 836-842.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yang, J. Li, J. Zhao, P. Ren, Z. Wang, B. Wei, B. Dong, R. Sun, X. Wang, H.J. Groen, Developing ultrasensitive library-aliquot-based droplet digital PCR for detecting T790M in plasma-circulating tumor DNA of non-small-cell-lung-cancer patients, Anal. Chem. 90 (2018) 11203-11209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Droplet microfluidics a tool for single cell analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.N. Joensson, H. Andersson Svahn, Droplet microfluidics a tool for single cell analysis, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 51 (2012) 12176-12192.
L. Shang, Y. Cheng, Y. Zhao, Emerging droplet microfluidics, Chem. Rev. 117 (2017) 7964-8040.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-cell analysis using drop-on-demand inkjet printing and probe electrospray ionization mass spectrometry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Chen, L. Lin, J. Zhang, Z. He, K. Uchiyama, J.-M. Lin, Single-cell analysis using drop-on-demand inkjet printing and probe electrospray ionization mass spectrometry, Anal. Chem. 88 (2016) 4354-4360.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A piezoelectric drop-on-demand generator for accurate samples in capillary electrophoresis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Chen, Y. Zhang, Y. Nakagawa, H. Zeng, C. Luo, H. Nakajima, K. Uchiyama, J.-M. Lin, A piezoelectric drop-on-demand generator for accurate samples in capillary electrophoresis, Talanta 107 (2013) 111-117.
Z. Zhan, J. An, Y. Wei, V.T. Tran, H. Du, Inkjet-printed optoelectronics, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 965-993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A FRET-based carbon dot-MnO2 nanosheet architecture for glutathione sensing in human whole blood samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, K. Jiang, J. Zhu, L. Zhang, H. Lin, A FRET-based carbon dot-MnO2 nanosheet architecture for glutathione sensing in human whole blood samples, Chem. Commun. 51 (2015) 12748-12751.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MnO2-nanosheet-modified upconversion nanosystem for sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose in blood

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yuan, Y. Cen, X.J. Kong, S. Wu, X. Chu, MnO2-nanosheet-modified upconversion nanosystem for sensitive turn-on fluorescence detection of H2O2 and glucose in blood, Acs Appl. Mater. Inter. 7 (2015) 10548.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Intelligent MnO2 nanosheets anchored with up- conversion nanoprobes for concurrent pH‐/H2O2‐responsive UCL imaging and oxygen‐elevated synergetic therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Fan, W. Bu, B. Shen, Q. He, Z. Cui, Y. Liu, X. Zheng, K. Zhao, Intelligent MnO2 nanosheets anchored with up- conversion nanoprobes for concurrent pH-/H2O2-responsive UCL imaging and oxygen-elevated synergetic therapy, J. Shi, Adv. Mater. 27 (2015) 4155-4161.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiple functional nanoprobe for contrast-enhanced bimodal cellular imaging and targeted therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-M. Meng, L. Lu, X.-H. Zhao, Z. Chen, Z. Zhao, C. Yang, X.-B. Zhang, W. Tan, Multiple functional nanoprobe for contrast-enhanced bimodal cellular imaging and targeted therapy, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 4448-4454.
